reasoning: The informal statement says (ZMod n, ·) is not a group for n > 1. Since ZMod n already carries a commutative monoid structure under multiplication (associativity and the identity 1 are automatic), the ONLY group axiom that can possibly fail is the existence of two-sided inverses. Hence "(ZMod n, ·) is a group" is logically equivalent to "every x ∈ ZMod n has a y with x·y = 1 and y·x = 1". Negating both sides gives exactly the Lean statement: ¬ (∀ x, ∃ y, x·y = 1 ∧ y·x = 1). The Lean formalization is logically equivalent to the informal claim under the standard reading, so it is strictly faithful.
